author: Valerii Shtuvbeinyi
text:
Dapr Agents v1.0 has officially reached General Availability at KubeCon Europe, marking a significant advancement for teams utilizing Dapr.

What it offers: a Python framework built on the Dapr runtime that enables AI agents to have durable workflows with auto-recovery, state persistence across over 30 databases, SPIFFE-based security between agents, and integrated observability. Additionally, it allows for switching LLM providers without the need to modify code.

The standout feature? It leverages your existing Dapr infrastructure. You can continue using the same pub/sub systems, state stores, and workflows now enhanced to support AI agents rather than just microservices.

ZEISS Vision Care showcased a production case at KubeCon, demonstrating the extraction of optical parameters from unstructured documents, this is not just a demo, but a real workload.

We operate Dapr on AKS for our cloud payroll system, and extending this infrastructure to AI agent, without the need for new middleware or state management, represents an evolution I am eager to test in practice.

Are you currently running Dapr? Is agent orchestration part of your future plans?

#Dapr #CloudArchitecture #AIAgents

🔗 Source: https://lnkd.in/deqFnPff
summary: Dapr Agents v1.0 GA announced at KubeCon with ZEISS production case

author: Anand Shangarpawar
text:
𝗗𝗮𝗽𝗿 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗱 𝗵𝗼𝘄 𝗜 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗸 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝗺𝗶𝗰𝗿𝗼𝘀𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗰𝗲𝘀. 𝗡𝗼𝘁 𝗯𝗲𝗰𝗮𝘂𝘀𝗲 𝗶𝘁'𝘀 𝗻𝗲𝘄 𝘁𝗲𝗰𝗵 𝗯𝘂𝘁 𝗯𝗲𝗰𝗮𝘂𝘀𝗲 𝗶𝘁 𝘀𝗼𝗹𝘃𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗿𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗺.

I've designed distributed systems for 10+ years. The plumbing never ends. Here's my honest breakdown of Dapr on a .NET project. No hype.

𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗗𝗮𝗽𝗿 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝗶𝘀 A sidecar runtime that exposes standard building blocks via HTTP or gRPC. Your app doesn't care what's behind them. Redis today. Azure Service Bus tomorrow. Your code? Unchanged.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝟴 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗯𝗹𝗼𝗰𝗸𝘀
➡️ Service invocation · retries, mTLS, discovery. No more manual Polly wiring.
🔄 Pub/sub · swap Kafka for RabbitMQ with one YAML change.
📑 State management · same API across Redis, CosmosDB, DynamoDB, Postgres.
♻️ Actors · virtual, stateful, single-threaded, auto-scaled.
⏲️ Workflow · durable, resumable processes. Think Durable Functions but portable.
🔑 Secrets & bindings · unified access to vaults, queues, files, HTTP endpoints.

𝗪𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗶𝘁 𝘄𝗶𝗻𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗶𝘁 𝗱𝗼𝗲𝘀𝗻'𝘁
✅ Green field on Kubernetes · deploy with Helm, inject sidecar, done.
✅ Multi-cloud portability · move from AWS SQS to Azure Service Bus: one YAML.
⚠️ Brownfield migration · sidecar adds complexity to lift-and-shifts. Plan carefully.
⚠️ Ultra-low latency paths · ~1ms sidecar hop. Benchmark before committing.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝗶𝗴𝗴𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗱𝘀𝗲𝘁 𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗳𝘁:
"Stop coupling your business logic to your infrastructure decisions." Your .NET service shouldn't know it's talking to Redis. That's an ops concern, not a code concern. Dapr enforces this at the runtime level.

𝟯 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘀 𝗜 𝘁𝗲𝗹𝗹 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 𝘁𝗲𝗮𝗺:
1️⃣ Read the component spec before writing a line of code. The YAML is where the power is.
2️⃣ Dapr Workflow (v1.2+) is underrated. Ditch hand-rolled saga orchestration.
3️⃣ Observability (W3C tracing + Prometheus) is excellent out of the box. Your ops team will thank you.

Dapr deserves a proper spike, not a toy demo. Stand it up in Docker Compose in an afternoon.
Drop a comment · which building block saved you the most pain? 👇

#dotnet #dapr #microservices #cloudnative #softwarearchitecture #kubernetes #distributedsystems #azure #devto #buildinpublic
summary: Detailed breakdown of Dapr building blocks for .NET microservices

author: Dustin Clonch
text:
Dapr Agents v1.0 just went GA under the Cloud Native Computing Foundation, and this is a big one.

This isn’t another experimental framework. It’s production infrastructure for agentic AI.

We’re talking about a Python-based system built for multi-agent coordination, state management, and failure recovery at scale. The exact pieces that have been missing while most companies stayed stuck in pilot mode.

The bottleneck isn’t model capability anymore. It’s orchestration.
How do agents hand off work reliably?
What happens when something fails mid-process?
How do you control behavior inside real production systems?

That’s the hard part. That’s the part most teams end up duct-taping together.

Frameworks like Dapr Agents change that. They give you a real foundation. Not just to experiment with agents, but to run them.

And that shift matters.

Because in 2026, the question isn’t “can AI agents work?”

It’s “can your business run them reliably at scale?”

The companies that get the infrastructure layer right will move faster, ship more, and operate differently than everyone else still stitching workflows together.

If you’re still treating agentic AI like a side experiment, you’re already behind.

https://lnkd.in/gYVx9g5y

#AIAgents #AgenticAI #EnterpriseAI #SoftwareDevelopment #Agilx
summary: Dapr Agents v1.0 GA as production infrastructure for agentic AI

author: Velmira Georgieva
text:
💳 charge_customer()
💾 update_database()
Not atomic.

Crash happens → money taken 💸, order stuck ⏳, customer calls 😬
Usual fixes (DB transactions, idempotency keys, sagas) have gaps.

✅ What worked: Dapr Workflow — checkpoints each step and resumes after crashes.

author: Yaron Schneider
text:
🚨 Big news for agent builders

We’ve just shipped a first-class integration between Dapr Workflows and LangChain Deep Agents. (Link in comments 👇)

This unlocks a crucial missing feature for Deep Agents:
👉 Fully durable agents - including the sandbox (!)
Not just checkpoints, no partial recovery.

With this integration:
🧠 All reasoning steps are durably persisted
🛠️ Every tool call is tracked and recoverable
🧪 The entire sandbox becomes durable
🔄 Execution resumes exactly where it left off

And importantly:
This works for tool execution both inside AND outside the virtual sandbox.

This joins our announcement from last week where we introduced durable workflow support to LangGraph, Google ADK, Strands Agents, Microsoft Agent Framework, CrewAI and OpenAI Agents. More to come 💪
summary: Dapr Workflows integrated with LangChain Deep Agents for durability

author: Gaurang M.
text:
If you’ve worked with microservices for a while, you’ve probably noticed something…

You keep writing the same infrastructure code again and again.
🔁 Retry logic
🗄️ Redis clients
🔐 Secrets fetching
📨 Pub/Sub integrations
🔎 Service discovery

Across every service.

That’s exactly the problem Dapr (Distributed Application Runtime) tries to solve.

It runs as a sidecar and exposes simple APIs for common distributed system patterns, making your services portable across AWS, Azure, GCP, or on-prem.

I recently wrote a deep-dive blog on Dapr where I cover:
📦 Dapr building block APIs
 ☁️ Multi-cloud portability with components
 ⚙️ Dapr Workflows for long-running processes
 🤖 Dapr Agents for AI-driven systems
 ☸️ Step-by-step Kubernetes setup
 💻 Python & Go code examples

If you're building microservices or distributed systems, you might find it useful.

👉 Blog link in the comments. Would love your thoughts! 🚀

#Dapr #Microservices #DistributedSystems #Kubernetes #DevOps

author: Sadia Mehmood
text:
Working with microservices sounded great… until integration became a nightmare.
Service-to-service calls, retries, message brokers, and state management — everything slowly started turning into boilerplate.

While working on a microservice-based system, I realized something:
I was spending more time solving distributed system problems than writing actual business logic.
Each of these challenges required separate tools, libraries, or infrastructure setups. The architecture was powerful — but the complexity kept growing.

That’s when I started exploring Dapr (Distributed Application Runtime).

What really stood out to me was its sidecar architecture.
Instead of every microservice implementing distributed system patterns itself, Dapr runs a sidecar alongside each service.
Your service simply communicates with the local Dapr sidecar, and the sidecar handles the heavy lifting across the distributed system.
All through consistent APIs, without every service having to reinvent the wheel.

The difference becomes very clear.

Without Dapr
• Custom service communication logic
• Manual retry and resiliency implementation
• Complex messaging integrations
• Multiple SDKs and infrastructure dependencies

With Dapr
• Sidecar handles service invocation
• Built-in pub/sub and resiliency
• Unified APIs for state and secrets
• Developers focus primarily on business logic

The biggest realization for me was this:

Dapr brings many microservice integration challenges under a single unified runtime, providing standardized building blocks that work across different platforms and infrastructures.

Microservices are incredibly powerful — but their operational complexity is real. Tools like Dapr help bridge the gap between scalability and developer productivity.

Curious to hear from other engineers —
Have you explored Dapr in your microservice architecture yet?
#Microservices #Dapr #SoftwareArchitecture #DistributedSystems #CloudNative
summary: Explains how Dapr sidecar architecture simplifies microservices

author: Ajay Mishra
text:
🚀 Simplifying Distributed Systems with 𝗗𝗮𝗽𝗿 𝗔𝗰𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘀

Distributed systems quickly become complex when you need to manage:

🔹 Distributed state
🔹 Concurrency
🔹 Thousands of independent entities

In my latest blog, I explore 𝗗𝗮𝗽𝗿 𝗔𝗰𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘀 with .𝗡𝗘𝗧 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗔𝘀𝗽𝗶𝗿𝗲 using a sample IoT fleet management application.

💡 Design idea: each device is modeled as an 𝗔𝗰𝘁𝗼𝗿.

Each device actor:

📡 Processes telemetry (𝗧𝗲𝗺𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲, 𝗔𝗤𝗜, 𝗢𝗰𝗰𝘂𝗽𝗮𝗻𝗰𝘆)
💾 Maintains its own state
🚨 Generates alerts when thresholds are crossed

The application was tested with 𝟱𝟬𝟬𝟬 𝗱𝗲𝘃𝗶𝗰𝗲𝘀 running as actors on a 𝗠𝗮𝗰𝗕𝗼𝗼𝗸, demonstrating how the Actor model can scale efficiently even in a local development setup.

Because actors own their state and process messages sequentially, the system scales naturally without complex locking or coordination logic.

⚙️ Meanwhile, 𝗗𝗮𝗽𝗿 handles the distributed system plumbing:

• Actor placement
• State persistence
• Activation & lifecycle management
• Timers and reminders

So developers can focus purely on business logic.

📖 Read the blog
https://lnkd.in/g79bA2qR

If you're exploring 𝗗𝗮𝗽𝗿, I’ve written several related posts covering different building blocks such as 𝗣𝘂𝗯𝗹𝗶𝘀𝗵/𝗦𝘂𝗯𝘀𝗰𝗿𝗶𝗯𝗲, 𝗦𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁, 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗔𝗣𝗜𝘀, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗸𝗳𝗹𝗼𝘄𝘀 all demonstrated using .𝗡𝗘𝗧 𝗔𝘀𝗽𝗶𝗿𝗲.

You can explore the full series here:
https://lnkd.in/gM2YWQnY

#Dapr #DotNet #Aspire #CloudNative #DistributedSystems
summary: Blog on Dapr Actors with .NET Aspire for IoT fleet management

author: Diagrid
text:
Missed the Dapr 1.17 celebration event? The full recording is now available!

Dapr co-creators, maintainers, and contributors walked through the newest features in the release and demoed what’s new across workflows, agents, and observability.

In the session we cover:
• Major improvements for workflows and agentic AI
• Workflow observability with Dash0
• Managing workflows with the Dapr CLI
• Workflow versioning with named workflows and patching
• Building resilient agentic systems with DurableAgent
• Agent observability with Dapr Agents and Grafana
• Response formatting and token usage with the Conversation API

Watch the full recording: https://hubs.ly/Q046ddtS0
#Dapr #AgenticAI #Agents #Workflows
summary: Dapr 1.17 celebration event recording covering workflows and agents

author: Mark Johnson
text:
Part 7 – Putting It All Together: A Real‑World Service with Dapr

Over the past six posts, we’ve explored Dapr’s core building blocks one by one: Running Dapr locally State management Pub/Sub Bindings and storage Observability Individually, each building block simplifies a specific problem. Together, they form a powerful pattern for building distributed systems where application code stays focused on business logic not infrastructure. In this final post, we’ll put everything together by walking through a simple but realistic service that uses multiple Dapr capabilities in a single workflow....
summary: Blog series finale building a real-world service with Dapr

author: Mark Fussell
text:
🚀 Announcing Durable Workflow for Agents

Agents shouldn’t restart from the beginning when they fail.

Today I am excited to announce that we’re launching durable workflows for AI agents. This bring integration between Dapr Workflow and the leading AI agent frameworks. Now developers can add reliable execution to popular agent frameworks including:

• LangGraph
• Microsoft Agent Framework
• AWS Strands
• Google Agent Development Kit (ADK)
• OpenAI Agents SDK
• CrewAI
• Pydantic AI Agents
• LangChain4J - Coming soon!

Here’s the problem we keep seeing:
AI agent frameworks are great at reasoning and orchestration.

But when something fails in production a timeout, process crash, or infrastructure network blip 💥 the agent restarts from the beginning.

Which means:
• repeating LLM calls costing you money
• rebuilding state and rerunning compute
• writing complex retry logic to handle failures or retrieve a last checkpoint an try to recover

So developers are stuck choosing between:
Workflow engines → reliability but no agent framework
Agent frameworks → great DX but fragile execution

With our new Diagrid extensions for Dapr Workflows you now get the best of both worlds.

Agents can now:
✅ Resume from the exact step that failed AND
✅ Automatically detect failed agent executions and resume them
✅ Keep all your existing agent code and logic

Agents in production need to do more than reason. They need to finish the job.

You can sign up for free and dive into our agent quickstarts with your favorite agent framework. See link in the comments below👇
summary: Announces durable workflow integrations for leading AI agent frameworks

author: KubeSphere Container Platform
text:
Cloud native continues to evolve rapidly, and this week’s roundup highlights several notable updates across the ecosystem. 😆 

In this edition, we look at:

▪️Dapr 1.17 and its enhanced workflow capabilities, performance, and operational improvements

▪️ Longhorn 1.11, where the V2 Data Engine enters Technical Preview
▪️A practical guide to running Apache Spark on Kubernetes
▪️And a closer look at Docker as a foundational force behind the container revolution

We also recommend several open-source projects worth exploring, including Freelens, Meshery, SpiceDB, and Testkube.

#CloudNative #Kubernetes #Docker #OpenSource #DevOps #PlatformEngineering
summary: Cloud native weekly roundup mentions Dapr 1.17 workflow improvements

author: Diego Alonso Espinosa Jimenez
text:
🏦 Saga Pattern PoC — Distributed Transactions with Dapr Workflow on Kubernetes

I just published a Proof of Concept demonstrating the Saga Pattern for managing distributed transactions across microservices — no distributed locks, no 2PC.

🔑 The Problem: In microservices, a simple bank transfer spans multiple services. If one step fails, how do you roll back consistently?

💡 The Solution: The Saga Pattern sequences local transactions with compensating transactions that automatically undo previous steps on failure.

🛠️ What I built:
Two Python microservices (FastAPI): an Account Service and a Saga Orchestrator
Dapr Workflow as the saga coordinator with durable execution
Dapr Service Invocation for inter-service communication via sidecars
Dapr State Management backed by Redis for account persistence
Zipkin for end-to-end distributed tracing
All running on Kubernetes (Minikube)
📊 Two flows demonstrated:
✅ Happy Path — Debit source → Credit destination → SUCCESS
❌ Error Path — Debit source → Credit fails → Compensate (reverse debit) → Balances restored

The beauty of Dapr Workflow is that compensation logic lives declaratively in the workflow definition — if the pod crashes mid-saga, it picks up right where it left off thanks to durable execution.

🔗 Check it out: https://lnkd.in/ePkWr8RU

#SagaPattern #Dapr #Microservices #Kubernetes #DistributedSystems #FastAPI #Python #CloudNative #DevOps #SoftwareArchitecture
summary: Saga Pattern PoC using Dapr Workflow for distributed transactions

author: FINTECH BELGIUM
text:
𝗪𝗲 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝘂𝗱 𝘁𝗼 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝗲 𝗥𝗘𝗗 𝗜𝗡𝗧𝗢 𝗚𝗥𝗘𝗘𝗡 𝗮𝘀 𝗮 𝗻𝗲𝘄 𝗽𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗻𝗲𝗿 𝗼𝗳 𝗙𝗶𝗻𝗧𝗲𝗰𝗵 𝗕𝗲𝗹𝗴𝗶𝘂𝗺!

RED INTO GREEN by DAPR is a RegTech solution helping financial institutions and #ICT providers turn complex EU regulations into structured, manageable compliance. RED INTO GREEN by DAPR specialises in digital resilience frameworks such as Digital Operational Resilience Act (#DORA), #NIS2 Directive, and General Data Protection Regulation (#GDPR). Their platform automates gap analysis, risk assessments, ICT third-party registers, and audit readiness, combining technology with regulatory expertise.

𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗙𝗶𝗻𝗧𝗲𝗰𝗵 𝗕𝗲𝗹𝗴𝗶𝘂𝗺?
We joined to support a strong, innovative fintech ecosystem where compliance and growth go hand in hand. We look forward to sharing expertise, exchanging best practices, and helping members turn regulatory pressure into sustainable advantage.

Curious how regulatory automation can simplify your processes? Message Mikołaj Otmianowski or Maria Błachł to start the conversation today.

#GRC #FTBelgium #RegTech
summary: RED INTO GREEN by DAPR is a RegTech company, not the runtime
